text: ) where DP (x * ) is an ( n – 1) ✕ ( n – 1) matrix called the linearized Poincaré map at x*. Since x * = P (x * ), we get v 1 = [ D P ( x * ) ] v 0 assuming that we can neglect the small O (║v 0 ║ 2 ) terms. The desired stability criterion is expressed in terms of the eigenvalues λ j . of DP ( x *): The closed orbit is linearly stable if and only if | λ j | < 1 for all j = 1, …, n -1. To understand this criterion, consider the generic case where there are no repeated eigenvalues. Then there is a basis of eigenvectors { e j } and so we can write v 0 = ∑ j = 1 n − 1 v j e j for some scalars v j Hence v 1 = ( D P ( x * ) ) ∑ j = 1 n − 1 v j e j = ∑ j = 1 n − 1 v j λ j e j Iterating the linearized map k times gives v k = ∑ j = 1 n − 1 v j ( λ j ) k e j . Hence, if all | λj | < 1, then ║ v k ║→ 0 geometrically fast. This proves that x * is linearly stable. Conversely, if | λ j . | > 1 for some j , then perturbations along e j grow, so x * is unstable. A borderline case occurs when the largest eigenvalue has magnitude | λ m | = 1; this occurs at bifurcations of periodic orbits, and then a nonlinear stability analysis is required. The λ j are called the characteristic or Floquet multipliers of the periodic orbit. (Strictly speaking, these are the nontrivial multipliers; there is always an additional trivial multiplier λ = 1 corresponding to perturbations along the periodic orbit. We have ignored such perturbations since they just amount to time-translation.) In general, the characteristic multipliers can only be found by numerical integration (see Exercise 8.7.10). The following examples are two of the rare exceptions. EXAMPLE 8.7.3: Find the characteristic multiplier for the limit cycle of Example 8.7.1 . Solution: We linearize about the fixed point r * = 1 of the Poincaré map. Let r = 1 + η , where η is infinitesimal. Then r ˙ = η ˙ = ( 1 + η ) ( 1 − ( 1 + η ) 2 ) . After neglecting O ( η 2 ) terms, we get η ˙ = − 2 η . Thus η ( t ) = η 0 e −2 t . After a time of flight t = 2π , the new perturbation is η 1 = e −4 π η 0 . Hence e −4 π is the characteristic multiplier. Since | e−4 π | < 1, the limit cycle is linearly stable. For this simple two-dimensional system, the linearized Poincaré map degenerates to a 1 × 1 matrix, i.e., a number. Exercise 8.7.1 asks you to show explicitly that P ’(r*) = e −4 π , as expected from the general theory above. Our final example comes from an analysis of coupled Josephson junctions. EXAMPLE 8.7.4: The N -dimensional system ϕ ˙ = Ω+ a sin ϕ i + 1 N ∑ J = i N sin ϕ J , (1) for i =1, ⋯, N, describes the dynamics of a series array of overdamped Josephson junctions in parallel with a resistive load (Tsang et al. 1991). For technological reasons, there is great interest in the solution where all the junctions oscillate in phase. This in-phase solution is given by ϕ 1 ( t ) = ϕ 2 ( t ) = … = ϕ N ( t ) = ϕ * ( t ) , where ϕ*( t ) denotes the common waveform. Find conditions under which the in-phase solution is periodic, and calculate the characteristic multipliers of this solution. Solution: For the in-phase solution, all N equations reduce to d ϕ * d t = Ω + ( a + 1 ) sin ϕ * (2) This has a periodic solution (on the circle) if and only if | Ω | > | α +1 |. To determine the stability of the in-phase solution, let ϕ t ( t ) = ϕ *( t ) + η i (t ), where the η i ( t ) are infinitesimal perturbations. Then substituting ∅ i . into (1) and dropping quadratic terms in η yields η ˙ i = [ a   cos ϕ * ( t ) ] η i + [ cos ϕ * ( t ) ] 1 N ∑ j = 1 N η j (3) We don’t have ϕ* ( t ) explicitly, but that doesn’t matter, thanks to two tricks. First, the linear system decouples if we change variables to μ = 1 N ∑ j = 1 N η j ,   ξ i = η i + 1 − η i ,   i = 1 … , N − 1 Then ξ ˙ i = [ a cos ϕ * ( t ) ] ξ i Separation of variables yields d ξ i ξ i = [ a cos ϕ * ( t ) ] d t = [ a cos ϕ * ] d ϕ * Ω + ( a + 1 ) sin ϕ * where we’ve used (2) to eliminate dt. (That was the second trick.) Now we compute the change in the perturbations after one circuit around the closed orbit ϕ *. ∮ d ξ i ξ i = a s ∫ 0 2 π [ a cos ϕ * ] d ϕ * Ω + ( a + 1 ) sin ϕ * ⇒ ln ξ i ( T ) ξ i
